other_observers: Other Observers on 9/19: Norm Erthal, Steve Stachowiak, Andrew Spencer, Mark Peterson, Peter Gent  Other Observers on 9/20: Glenn Walbek, Andrew Spencer, Hugh and Urling Kingery; Original observers: Joey Kellner, Paul Differding, and Vince Kloster.  Within three hours: Bill Maynard, Kara Lewantowicz, Rachel Hopper, Bill Schmoker, Joe Roller, Emily Steel, Mim Ackley.
duration: 50 minutes of bird viewing over a five hour period
specific_location: Northwest side of Prewitt Reservoir (Prewitt State Wildlife Area).
description: Medium-sized shorebird, appeared to be ever so slightly shorter that nearby Stilt Sandpipers (a little shorter legged). A medium-brown, streaked cap on the head. Auriculars medium buff. Large, dark brown lores extended back barely past the eyes making the bright whitish supercillium really stand out. Throat white, light buff on front of neck and upper breast. Lower breast, belly and undertail coverts STARK white (seen as the bird faced away from me and fed); Legs, bill and eyes black. Bill longish and evenly decurved the entire length (not straight, then drooping at the tip like the Stilt Sandpipers); back, scapulars, wing coverts and tertials were medium-brown with chrisp white feather edges on scaps and wing coverts (identifying this bird as a juvenile). Thin white wingbar running along the base of the primaries and secondaries starting at the body, running along the center of the wing and extending the length of the wing (seen as the bird preened and twice when the bird flew). Primaries dark brown and slightly longer than the tail; Rump white, tail dark brown or black; Did not notice the leg length when the bird flew.Behaviors: Witnessed this bird: 1) Feeding "sewing machine" style just like nearby Stilt Sandpiper. While feeding with Stilt Sandpipers and a few Long-billed Dowitchers this bird was almost always on the shore side of the flock and not in deeper water on the backside of the flock. 2) Preening breast, belly and wings giving observers a good look at the all-white rump and wingbar. 3) Flying with Stilt, Baird's and Pectoral Sandpipers and Long-billed Dowitchers allowing a great comparison with these (if you could find it in the flock!) and an excellent view of the wingbar.  Bird flushed several times and almost always stayed in the company of anywhere from five to about forty Stilt Sandpipers.  Last seen when a Peregrine Falcon came in and made several passes at a Lesser Yellowlegs (which it eventually caught and ate) and flush all the birds. May have flown to the far south shore of the reservoir.Call: None notedPlumage: juvenile
photographer: Photographer: Joey Kellner, Joey Kellner, Joey Kellner, Joey Kellner, Joey Kellner
